title: Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist

learningObjectives.text: Explain limits of manual dom programming using a new example and justify the result.
Explain declarative ui using a new example and justify the result.
Explain react's component model using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: This topic turns why front-end frameworks and react exist from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **React Orientation** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Limits of manual DOM programming** → **Declarative UI** → **React's component model**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Limits of manual DOM programming
sections.body: As interfaces grow, manually synchronizing DOM nodes with application state creates scattered update logic and inconsistent states. A declarative framework centralizes the relationship between data and UI.

sections.title: Declarative UI
sections.body: Declarative UI expresses what should appear for current props and state. React recalculates an element tree and reconciles changes instead of requiring a manual sequence of DOM mutations.

sections.title: React's component model
sections.body: React applications are composed from functions that describe UI from inputs. Components create local boundaries, can be nested, and expose a public API through props and composition.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The component describes UI for a score instead of manually mutating DOM.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist connects Limits of manual DOM programming, Declarative UI, React's component model.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Limits of manual DOM programming” in the context of Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist?

choices.label: As interfaces grow, manually synchronizing DOM nodes with application state creates scattered update logic and inconsistent states. A declarative framework centralizes the relationship between data and UI.
choices.feedback: Limits of manual DOM programming is best understood as follows: As interfaces grow, manually synchronizing DOM nodes with application state creates scattered update logic and inconsistent states. A declarative framework centralizes the relationship between data and UI.

choices.label: React assumes comfort with JavaScript values, functions, arrays, objects, modules, and asynchronous code. Missing prerequisites are review signals, not barriers; revisit the smallest required concept and return.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Limits of manual DOM programming.

choices.label: Use state for changing information that affects rendering, ordinary variables for render calculations, and refs for mutable non-rendered values.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Limits of manual DOM programming.

choices.label: Keep dependencies complete and stabilize values only where identity is genuinely required.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Limits of manual DOM programming.

questions.explanation: Limits of manual DOM programming is best understood as follows: As interfaces grow, manually synchronizing DOM nodes with application state creates scattered update logic and inconsistent states. A declarative framework centralizes the relationship between data and UI.
questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Apply Limits of manual DOM programming deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist?

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Limits of manual DOM programming, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist?

choices.label: Use Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist.

choices.label: Avoid Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist.

choices.label: Use Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Why Front-End Frameworks and React Exist.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
